sections.paragraph:
Pl. זְנָבֹות, constr. זַנְבֹות the tail of animals (Arab. ذَنَبُ and ذَنَبَةُ Syr. ܕܘܽܢܒܐܳ id. The verb ذنب to follow after, is secondary). Exo 4:4 Jud. 15:4 Job 40:17. Metaphorically, extremity, the end of any thing. שְׁנֵי זַנְבֹות הָאוּדִים “two ends of fire-brands,” Isa 7:4. Also something vile, or contemptible, especially as opposed to רֹאשׁ Deu 28:13, “Jehovah will make thee the head and not the tail;” verse 44 Deu 28:44. Isa 9:13 19:15 . (In the same sense the Arabs oppose انف وذنب nose and tail, see my commentary on Isa 9:13.) Hence the denominative verb-

Piel זִנֵּב properly to hurt, or cut off the tail, hence figuratively to smite, or rout the rear of a host (Arab. ذنب, Greek οὐρά, οὐραγία). Deu 25:18 Jos 10:10. Denominative verbs derived from the names of members of the body often have the sense in the Phœnicio-Shemitic languages of hurting or cutting off those members. See Lehrg. p. 257, and Ewald’s Hebr. Gram. p. 200.
